interrogator_dossier:
# TUI AG (TUI1.DE) — Forensic Dossier

**Analyst:** Interrogator (Opus 4.8) · **Signal:** deep_value · **Sector:** Consumer Cyclical
**Transcript window:** Q3-FY25 (2025-08-13) → Q2/H1-FY26 (2026-05-13), 4 calls · **Financials:** screener scan + FMP TTM to 2026-03-31

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these four calls is one of a post-COVID turnaround company shifting its self-description from "recovery" to "transformation-and-now-monetize." The constant refrain is **vertical integration**: TUI repeatedly frames itself not as a tour operator but as an "integrated" travel platform where a "strong distribution" arm (Markets + Airline) exists chiefly to "fill our assets" (the high-margin Holiday Experiences segment — Hotels & Resorts, Cruises, Musement). By the FY25 results call (Dec-2025), Ebel states the punchline explicitly: "more than 80% of the profit come from Holiday Experiences and only 15% from Markets & Airline."

The arc has three legs:

1. **Q3-FY25 (Aug-2025):** Offensive, confident. "Q3 delivering the best ever result," guidance *raised* to 9–11% EBIT growth (constant currency). Heavy emphasis on growth optionality — Tours product rollout ("our natural market share should be 30%" vs ~3% today), app/AI, new source markets (Portugal, Spain, Italy, Americas), Africa hotel expansion.

2. **FY25 results (Dec-2025):** The pivot to capital return. Record FY, and crucially the **dividend policy is unveiled** (10–20% of underlying EPS) after years of no payout. New FY26 guidance of **7–10% EBIT growth**. But a quieter admission surfaces: Markets & Airline EBIT *fell ~€100m* on transformation/IT spend, provisions, "peak of IT investment." The growth story is now explicitly back-end-loaded to summer and dependent on "commercializing the airline."

3. **Q1 then Q2-FY26 (Feb→May 2026):** Defensive. Two exogenous shocks — the **Jamaica (Hurricane Melissa)** hotel/flight closures and, far more material, the **Iran war / Strait of Hormuz** episode that trapped two cruise ships for ~10 weeks and forced repatriation flights. Guidance was *cut* in April 2026 to a wide **€1.10bn–€1.40bn** underlying EBIT band, with revenue guidance suspended. Management reconfirms but concedes "visibility is not as much as we are used to." The narrative quietly shifts from "more customers, more products" (offense) to "right capacity," "risk-adverse," "protect margin," "manage short-term bookings" (defense).

Growth drivers *introduced*: Tours/round-trips, OneWeb commercial airline platform, AI in app/distribution, River Cruises, Oman/Africa/Asia hotel clusters, dynamic packaging (toward 20% of mix). Drivers *quietly de-emphasized*: outright volume growth ("another 1 or 2 volume with lower margin doesn't help us"), the Markets & Airline top line (now a cost-synergy story, not a revenue story).

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "record performance," "fourth consecutive year with significant profit growth" | Dec-25 | NI: -213M (FY22) → 306M (FY23) → 507M (FY24) → 636M (FY25); EPS 0.75→0.98→1.14 | **Corroborated.** Genuine, monotonic earnings recovery. |
| "best ever" Q3; guidance raised to 9–11% | Aug-25 | EPS +16.3% YoY, beat 5/7 qtrs (71%) | **Corroborated** at the time. |
| "strong increase in EBIT," integrated model delivering | Dec-25 | Revenue +4.4% YoY (decelerating from 3-yr CAGR +13.5%); gross margin only **9.2%**, net **2.6%** | **Partial.** EBIT/NI up, but the absolute margin is razor-thin and revenue growth is normalizing hard as the recovery base laps out. |
| Balance sheet "step improvement," net debt down €0.5bn | Feb-26 | Scan net_debt 1.41B; **LIVE CORRECTION: true net debt 3.01B** (4.47B debt − 1.46B cash, 2026-03-31); web: ~€3.6bn Dec-25 | **Directionally true but the level is still heavy.** Altman-Z **1.4** = distress zone. Deleveraging is real (net lev 0.6x→<0.5x target) but the company is not yet de-risked. |
| ROE 99% (quality signal) | scan | **ROIC 0.4%** | **The tell.** 99% ROE is an artifact of a thin/negative-equity base post-restructuring, NOT franchise quality. ROIC ~0% means the business as a whole does not earn its cost of capital. |
| "Cruise is absolutely the strongest unit," 22%/17% returns, ~100% occupancy | Dec/May | Not separately in scan, but consistent with the HEX-drives-80%-of-profit claim | **Credible** — this is the genuine crown jewel and the SoP anchor. |
| FY26 EBIT +7–10% growth | Dec-25→ | **Cut to €1.10–1.40bn in Apr-26** on Iran; FCF -17.4% YoY, TTM FCF €722M | **Missed/derisked.** The growth guide survived ~4 months before exogenous shocks forced a wide-band cut. FCF is shrinking, not growing. |

The core divergence: management narrates a *quality compounder* ("superior," "outstanding," "best of breed"), but the consolidated financials describe a *thin-margin, capital-heavy, still-levered cyclical* whose only above-cost-of-capital economics live inside the Holiday Experiences segment. The story is **richer than the consolidated numbers justify** — but the *segment* story (HEX) is real, which is exactly why this is a sum-of-parts case, not a whole-company-multiple case.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING** (from offensive-confident to defensive-cautious), though the *operational* tone on the crown-jewel segments stays high.

- **Aug-25 (Rising/peak):** "good mood," "best ever result," guidance *raised*. Pure offense.
- **Dec-25 (Confident but hedging):** "excellent year," "record," dividend initiated — but the first defensive notes: Germany "in a very difficult situation… I don't see any positive impact to any business in Germany"; winter "more difficult to predict than it has been in recent years," "we are cautious."
- **Feb-26 (Steady, absorbing shocks):** "best start," reaffirms 7–10%, but now narrating one-offs (Jamaica) and interest-cost wins rather than demand.
- **May-26 (Defensive):** "challenging market environment," guidance band widened, "visibility is not as much as we are used to," "we have been more risk adverse," "short-term, the business will be challenging." The language pivots decisively from growth/products to capacity discipline and margin protection.

Specificity on guidance actually *decreased* — from a tight "9–11%" to a €300m-wide EBIT band with suspended revenue guidance. That widening band is the single clearest confidence tell.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM.**

The beat rate (5/7 ≈ 71%) and the multi-year delivery (4 straight years of profit growth, EPS 0.75→1.14) are genuinely good and argue for credibility. Management *raised* guidance mid-FY25 and delivered a record year — that is the bull's strongest credibility point.

But the FY26 7–10% growth guide, set in Dec-25, was cut to a wide band by Apr-26. To be fair, the cause was **exogenous** (Iran war / Hormuz, Jamaica hurricane) rather than operational miss — management was transparent and quantified it (Q2 Iran impact "€40 million plus," another €20m Cruise, total H2 hit toward the €50m+ they flagged). This is honest disclosure, not a credibility breach. The pattern is **not sandbagging** — if anything they guided optimistically into a shock. Net: they deliver on what they control, but the thesis is repeatedly hostage to macro/geopolitics they cannot, and the wide reaffirmed band signals real uncertainty about H2 summer bookings.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"Why aren't cruise prices rising despite huge demand?"** (Bernstein, UBS, BofA, repeatedly.) Management concedes the point candidly — "no one and especially not me anticipated that the demand would be stronger than the capacity increase… we could probably have raised the price by 3%, 4% more." A genuine, admitted left-on-the-table issue; the upside is real but the *timing* of capturing it ("the big impact will be in the coming years") keeps slipping forward.
- **Markets + Airline EBIT bridge** (UBS/Nedelcu pressed hard): analysts want the moving parts of how M&A grows EBIT off €200m with bookings down ~2% and cost inflation. Management answers with "transformation"/"commercialization" qualitatively, not a hard bridge — a recurring soft spot.
- **CapEx and Marella on/off balance sheet** (Barclays): probing whether new-ship funding stays on B/S (it does — ships taken into ownership). Relevant to the leverage thesis.
- **Payment terms competition** (UBS): management visibly resistant — "the last escape… I would do my utmost to not agree to that" — flagging competitive pressure on working capital they don't want to engage.
- **German consumer weakness:** management volunteers candor ("I don't see any positive impact to any business in Germany"), a structural demand worry in its largest source market that the growth narrative otherwise glosses.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **ROIC ~0.4% / Altman-Z 1.4** — consolidated returns do not clear cost of capital; balance sheet still in the statistical distress band despite deleveraging.
🔴 **Guidance cut + suspended revenue guide + widened band** within 4 months of being set — visibility is genuinely impaired into the key summer.
🔴 **Geopolitical fragility** — two separate exogenous shocks (Jamaica, Iran/Hormuz) in two quarters; the business is structurally exposed to oil/conflict/weather, and the *current* regime (live Iran war, WTI ~$92) is exactly its worst tail.
🔴 **Cruise pricing repeatedly left on the table** — execution gap on monetizing the one undeniable demand strength; the upside keeps being deferred "to coming years."
🔴 **Mordashov 24.9% stake frozen** under EU sanctions — a permanent, unresolvable overhang and governance dead-weight; no activist/strategic-process path while frozen.
🔴 **FCF shrinking** (-17.4% YoY; 3-yr CAGR -14.1%) even as EPS rises — heavy CapEx (Boeing MAX fleet, Marella ships, hotels) consumes the earnings.

🟢 **Dividend reinstated** (Feb-2026 AGM) after years — a real balance-sheet-confidence signal, and a discipline anchor (10–20% of EPS, deliberately modest to keep deleveraging).
🟢 **Deleveraging is real and verified** — net debt €3.6bn Dec-25, net lev 0.6x→<0.5x target; all three agencies back to BB/Ba (Moody's Ba3 *positive* outlook Feb-26); IG is a credible 12–24mo target.
🟢 **Holiday Experiences is a genuine quality engine** — 84–88% hotel occupancy, ~100% cruise occupancy, 22%/17% cruise returns, 35m-customer ecosystem with low-cost owned distribution and ~40% retention. This is the SoP anchor and it is improving.
🟢 **Honest, quantified disclosure** of one-offs; 4 straight years of profit growth; 71% beat rate.
🟢 **Self-help levers still loaded** — airline commercialization (OneWeb), €250m cost program, dynamic packaging toward 20%, cruise price catch-up — structural, management-controlled, not macro-dependent.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **"Risk capacity" → "right capacity"/"risk-adverse":** a deliberate vocabulary shift from a growth posture to a capital-preservation posture. Repeated reductions in third-party (non-owned) risk capacity signal management is shrinking the low-margin tour-operator tail and concentrating on owned-asset fill — structurally margin-accretive but volume-dilutive (explains decelerating revenue).
- **AI framing escalates:** from "we bring AI into the search" (Aug-25) to "the most disruptive part… in the world… a way of how we work… new, more cost-effective distribution channels" (May-26). Notably TUI frames AI as a *cost/distribution ally*, not a threat — plausible for an owned-product/experiences business (AI doesn't replace a hotel bed or a cruise berth), unlike for thin-switching-cost OTAs.
- **A new COO layer** installed (May-26) "beneath me" to drive cost/efficiency synergies and load factors — an organizational tell that the next leg is execution/efficiency, not expansion.
- **Capital allocation language is consistently deleverage-first:** dividend "balancing deleverage targets, investment… decent returns" — the order is the message. This is a credit-recovery story wearing an equity-growth costume.
- **Fleet disadvantage admitted:** "60 in the summer out of 100, 737 MAX… we don't have a cost competitive product to the A321neo" — a candid cost-structure gap vs. peers that will take "another year" to close.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE, tilting STRENGTHENING on the balance sheet but with DETERIORATING near-term visibility.** The deleveraging/dividend trajectory is genuinely improving (credit recovery toward IG); the operating-confidence trajectory is declining under exogenous shocks into the make-or-break summer.

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** This is a **sum-of-parts** name, not a multiple-on-the-whole name. The consolidated 3.8x P/FCF and ~0% ROIC describe a thin cyclical; the *value* lives almost entirely in Holiday Experiences (Hotels/Riu, Cruises/TUI Cruises+Marella, Musement), which is a real quality asset partly masked by a loss-leading Markets+Airline tail and €3bn net debt. The director must decide whether the HEX SoP value, net of debt and the still-live Iran/oil tail, clears a margin of safety at ~€7.3 — and must NOT credit the optical 3.8x as if it were the franchise multiple. The load-bearing *catalysts have largely already fired* (dividend reinstated; guidance reset); what remains is a slow deleveraging-to-IG grind exposed to a geopolitical tail that is currently active.

**ECONOMIC MOAT:** **NARROW, STABLE.** Moat type = intangible-brand + vertical-integration scale within European package travel (TUI brand, owned hotels/ships, 35m-customer distribution ecosystem with genuine low-cost owned-product funnel and ~40% retention). It is *narrow* — capital-intensive, cyclical, thin-margin (9.2% gross), with ROIC that does not clear WACC at the consolidated level — but it is **not eroding**: HEX occupancy, rates, and returns are stable-to-rising, and the integration funnel is a genuine, defensible cost edge. The brand demonstrably created value in the Iran repatriation episode ("strong brands always benefit from a crisis"). It is not WIDE (no pricing-power-driven excess returns at the group level) and not ERODING (the durable HEX base is holding/improving).

**SECULAR THREAT:** **MANAGEABLE / none material.** The plausible secular force is AI/OTA disintermediation of travel distribution (ai-displacement / retail-channel-shift), but TUI's value is anchored in *owned physical assets* (hotel beds, cruise berths) and *owned products* that AI cannot commoditize — and TUI is positioning AI as a distribution-cost *tailwind*, with early evidence (app conversion, Musement B2B wins with Booking/easyJet/Jet2). The genuine threats are *cyclical, not terminal*: European consumer weakness (esp. Germany), geopolitical/oil shocks, and capital intensity. The cheapness is therefore primarily a **fear-print on a cyclical + a levered, complex SoP**, not a structurally-shrinking base — i.e. closer to mispricing than value trap, conditioned on the Iran tail clearing and the deleveraging continuing.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: STABLE | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: STABLE | SECULAR_THREAT: manageable

bull_thesis: TUI is a genuine operational turnaround that the market is pricing as if it were still in distress. The shares trade at just 3.7 times free cash flow (the cash the business generates after running costs and investment) because investors still anchor to the near-wipeout of the COVID years, when the company lost about EUR 2.48 billion in 2021. Since then, profit has recovered steadily: net income rose from EUR 306 million in 2023 to EUR 507 million in 2024 to EUR 636 million in 2025, with earnings per share climbing from EUR 0.75 to EUR 0.98 to EUR 1.14. The company has now grown its underlying operating profit for 14 quarters in a row and has beaten expectations 71% of the time. The crown jewel is the Holiday Experiences arm (hotels, the cruise joint venture, and the Musement experiences platform), which delivers more than 80% of group profit on a capital-light model: hotels run at 84% to 88% occupancy, cruises at roughly 100%, with cruise returns on invested capital of 22% and 17%, all fed cheaply by a first-party database of 35 million customers. There is large, clearly identified upside that has not yet shown up in the numbers: management believes it loses 20% to 30% of demand because, in its own words, it has not run the airline like an airline, and the commercial fix is only now being rolled out in Belgium and the Netherlands before the UK. A single global technology and AI platform cost program is due to be detailed in December 2026, and management has admitted the cruise business is under-priced, noting it could probably have raised prices 3% to 4% more even with capacity already 100%-plus sold for next year. The dividend has been reinstated, consensus expects forward earnings-per-share growth of 31%, and two intrinsic-value methods both point well above EUR 7: a discounted cash flow model (which values the business on its projected future cash) shows an 81% margin of safety, and an earnings-power model (which values it on sustainable current earnings) shows 49%. The case holds if European discretionary spending stays firm, there is no fresh geopolitical or weather shock, and the airline improvement converts over the summer of the 2026 financial year.
bear_thesis: TUI is a thin-margin, capital-hungry, still-distressed cyclical, and its cheap price reflects real business risk rather than a mistake by the market. On a funded basis, where you count all the debt the company actually owes, net debt is about EUR 3.0 billion (EUR 4.47 billion of total debt minus EUR 1.46 billion of cash), not the roughly EUR 1.3 billion management quotes using a narrower definition that leaves out leases. Its Altman-Z score of 1.4, a standard bankruptcy-risk gauge where anything below 1.8 signals distress, sits firmly in the danger zone. The 3.7-times free cash flow valuation rests on a free cash flow line that is falling, down 17% over the year and down 14% a year over three years, because the investment cycle is eating the profit recovery: 60 of 100 Boeing MAX aircraft still to be delivered, Marella cruise ships taken into ownership, a 70-hotel pipeline, and lease buy-backs that add to funded debt. Group-level returns are close to nothing, with returns on invested capital of 0.4%, gross margin of 9.2%, and net margin of 2.6%; the headline 99% return on equity is an illusion created by an equity base that was almost wiped out, not a sign of quality. The best segment, cruise, is largely a 50/50 joint venture with Royal Caribbean, so its value reaches TUI only as a dividend and does not pay down the parent's debt. Management's tone has turned defensive, talking about being risk-averse and protecting margin, with summer bookings down 2% and customers booking late. Guidance for the 2026 financial year (EUR 1.10 billion to EUR 1.40 billion, a EUR 300 million range management itself calls low-visibility) was cut in April 2026 after the Iran war and the Jamaica hurricane. The catalysts that could re-rate the stock have already happened (the dividend in December 2025 and February 2026, the guidance raise in August 2025), the shares have already rallied from about EUR 7.08 to EUR 7.7, and the market is already bullish, with price targets near EUR 10.4 implying about 46% upside and Jefferies moving back to neutral after the run, so there is no negative positioning left to squeeze. A soft summer report, a currency, jet-fuel, or geopolitical knock, or another missed airline target would re-open the distress discount on a business with a 1.4 Altman-Z score and falling free cash flow.
consensus_delta: For a value name, the key question is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a business with stable or growing earnings, and here it is not. The market is already bullish: consensus 12-month price targets sit around EUR 10.4 to EUR 10.45 across roughly 13 analysts rating it outperform, implying about 46% upside, with JPMorgan and Deutsche Bank at buy and overweight, while Jefferies has just moved back to neutral after the rally. So the gap between our view and the market's is thin to none: the recovery, the dividend reinstatement, and the EUR 1.1 billion to EUR 1.4 billion ambition for the 2026 financial year are already baked into expectations. The one genuine difference is how leverage is read. Many sell-side models carry management's underlying net debt of about EUR 1.3 billion (0.6 times earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization), whereas the funded balance sheet shows about EUR 3.0 billion of net debt and a distress-zone Altman-Z score of 1.4. But that gap is a risk the market is underweighting, not upside it is missing. In short, this is not a name where the market is looking the wrong way; it is a well-covered mid-cap recovery whose re-rating has largely already happened.
forcing_function: There is no hard, dated event that forces the re-rating, and for a value thesis that is the defining weakness. The events that could lift the stock have already happened: the dividend was announced on 10 December 2025 and paid at the February 2026 annual meeting, the August 2025 guidance raise is spent, and the April 2026 guidance revision was downward. What remains is soft and recurring: the December 2026 capital-markets-day detail on the cost program (a self-set, soft date) and the perennial hope that summer demand will be strong, which is a seasonal wish rather than an event. The next real data point is third-quarter results for the 2026 financial year in August 2026, but that is a reading, not a structural turning point. Given the current market backdrop, which rewards hard-dated special situations in thinly covered corners and penalizes theses that hinge on one shared macro factor or on a rescue from the consumer or interest rates, TUI is exactly the kind of name to deprioritize: a well-covered cyclical whose outcome depends on the same European-consumer and oil-and-jet-fuel factors as everything else, with no independent driver to resolve it.
valley_of_death: The next 3 to 9 months, covering the second half of the 2026 financial year and the summer season, are the risky window. First, summer 2026 demand is the whole game, and bookings were down 2% with management admitting low visibility and a late-booking consumer; a soft July-to-September period hits the EUR 1.10 billion to EUR 1.40 billion guidance directly. Second, there is a residual drag from the Iran and Strait of Hormuz disruption: management guided to roughly EUR 20 million-plus of further cruise cost, with Middle East and Far East demand almost at zero and normalizing only by year-end, and any fresh spike in oil or jet fuel (the live oil risk in today's market) would compound it. Third, free cash flow faces a hump because capital spending stays high (the MAX fleet roll-out, Marella ships now on the balance sheet, the hotel pipeline), so free cash flow, already down 17% over the year, is likely to stay under pressure through the investment peak. Fourth, currency: a stronger euro against the pound translates UK summer earnings lower, something the finance chief flagged explicitly. With an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4 and EUR 3 billion of residual net debt, any one of these can widen the distress discount again before the airline turnaround has a chance to convert.
positioning_washout: There is limited forced-selling pressure in either direction. The dividend is a token EUR 0.10 per share (about a 1.4% yield) and the policy is only 10% to 20% of underlying earnings per share, so there is no large income-focused holder base to flush out, and no dividend cut pending that would force index funds to sell. The shareholder base is recovery and cyclical-momentum money that has already been pulled in by the rally from about EUR 7.08 to EUR 7.7, which is itself the risk: it is fast money that exits on a soft summer report, so the selling risk is to the downside on any demand wobble rather than a contrarian buying setup. There is no index-inclusion or forced-buying catalyst to point to. In short, positioning offers no asymmetric pain trade to harvest.
moderator_conclusion: The recommendation is to pass for now, with low conviction (a 2 on our scale). The operational recovery is real and well-evidenced (net income rising from EUR 306 million to EUR 636 million, 14 straight quarters of operating-profit growth, and an attractive capital-light Holiday Experiences arm), and the value case stands on its own, which is why our value conviction is a 3, deliberately set above the catalyst-aware conviction. But as a special situation judged against the current market backdrop, it falls short on four counts. First, the catalysts that mattered have already happened (the dividend reinstatement and the August 2025 guidance raise), so the re-rating is largely spent and the stock has already rallied. Second, the market is already bullish (price targets near EUR 10.4, about 46% upside, Jefferies back to neutral), so there is no negative positioning to squeeze, the opposite of what a deep-value setup needs. Third, the thesis hinges on the single shared macro factor the current backdrop penalizes, namely European discretionary demand plus oil and jet fuel (the Hormuz risk), with no independent resolver and no hard, dated catalyst, only a soft December 2026 cost-program reveal and the hope that summer will be strong. Fourth, the balance sheet is still distressed: funded net debt of about EUR 3.0 billion (not management's roughly EUR 1.3 billion), an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4, and free cash flow falling 17%, so the equity is a thin, geared sliver and the base-case risk and reward (about EUR 8.5 fair value against EUR 4.5 to EUR 6.6 on the downside) is roughly even, not the lopsided payoff the book requires. The competitive advantage is narrow (a genuine vertical-integration and first-party-distribution edge in Holiday Experiences, but group returns on invested capital of just 0.4% and a 2.6% net margin) and widening off a low base, with a manageable, cyclical-not-terminal long-term profile, so neither the moat nor any secular threat caps the value conviction; it is held at 3 by the leverage and the spent catalyst, not capped by them. If you want this name, the backdrop-consistent way to play it is to wait for a summer-demand sell-off or an oil or currency shock to re-open the distress discount toward the EUR 4.5 to EUR 6 bear-case value, in other words add it to a watchlist for a wash-out. At today's price, with the catalyst spent and the market already paid, the call is to pass.
sop_breakdown: There are about 558 million shares. The base case assumes underlying operating profit of roughly EUR 1.2 billion, the midpoint of the EUR 1.10 billion to EUR 1.40 billion guidance for the 2026 financial year. Valuing the business piece by piece (a sum-of-the-parts approach, where each division is valued separately): (1) Holiday Experiences at about EUR 1.0 billion of underlying operating profit on a blended 7.5 times enterprise value to operating profit (between the bull case of 8.5 times and the bear case of 6.5 times, with a haircut because the cruise crown jewel is an equity-method joint venture rather than consolidated cash) is worth about EUR 7.5 billion. (2) Markets and Airline at about EUR 0.2 billion on roughly 4.25 times (the turnaround is real but not yet delivered, and the comparison is discounted for tour-operator risk) is worth about EUR 0.85 billion. That gives a gross enterprise value of about EUR 8.35 billion. Subtracting funded net debt of EUR 3.0 billion leaves equity of about EUR 5.35 billion, or roughly EUR 9.6 per share on a clean read. Taking a 10% to 12% haircut for understated lease and capital-spending obligations and the distressed 1.4 Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score brings the base value to about EUR 8.5. This sits between the earnings-power estimate (a 49% margin of safety, about EUR 10.7) on the high side and the funded-debt-burdened bear estimate (about EUR 6.6) on the low side. The stock-page discounted-cash-flow figure of EUR 38 is rejected as an outlier, inconsistent with a business earning a 2.6% net margin and 0.4% returns on invested capital.
risk_reward: The shares trade around EUR 7.16. The downside to where the bear case breaks is about EUR 4.5 to EUR 6.6 (the bear sum-of-the-parts value, or -8% to -37%), against upside to fair value of about EUR 8.5 in the base case and about EUR 12.8 in the bull case (+19% and +79%). That makes the base-case payoff roughly even, about 1.0 to 1.3 times upside to fair value against about 1 times downside to the break point, not the lopsided payoff a deep-value setup wants, because EUR 3 billion of net debt makes the equity a thin, geared sliver and the re-rating catalysts have already fired. The upside needs both the airline turn and firm summer demand, neither of which is banked, while the downside requires only a wobble in demand against a 1.4 Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score.
peer_comps_note: The peer comparison flags TUI as cheap against rivals (3.7 times free cash flow versus roughly 12 to 15 times for Ryanair and about 15 times for Booking Holdings), but that read is heavily discounted here for two reasons. First, TUI is an integrated tour-operator, airline, hotel, and cruise conglomerate, not a pure online travel agency or an asset-light hotelier, so comparing its free cash flow multiple to Booking or Ryanair is apples to oranges: its 9.2% gross margin, 2.6% net margin, and 0.4% returns on invested capital are structurally weaker and justify a large discount. Second, the peer data itself notes the catch, calling it cheap on free cash flow multiples but with genuine business-risk offset: an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.37 in distress territory, plus leverage, airline overcapacity, and dependence on discretionary spending. So the discount to online-travel and airline peers is largely compensation for funded leverage and capital intensity, not hidden outperformance. With no peer override or anchor multiple supplied, the valuation here rests on segment-level enterprise value to operating profit (Holiday Experiences at about 7.5 times, Markets and Airline at about 4.25 times), which is the defensible relative-value lever, rather than a whole-company free cash flow screen that flatters a falling-cash-flow, EUR 3 billion net-debt balance sheet.
interrogator_dossier:
# A deep dive on management and the setup — TUI AG (TUI1.DE)

*Integrated European travel group: tour operator, airline, hotels (TUI Blue, Robinson, the Riu joint venture), cruises (the TUI Cruises joint venture, Hapag-Lloyd, Marella), and Musement experiences. The earnings-call transcripts span the third quarter of the 2025 financial year (August 2025) through the second quarter and first half of the 2026 financial year (May 2026). This is a value-style setup.*

## 1. How the story has evolved

The story across these five calls is remarkably consistent in message and shifts from rising confidence to growing caution. The spine never changes: vertical integration plus a global technology and AI platform fills the company's own assets (hotels at 84% to 88% occupancy, cruises at roughly 100%), and that funnel is the profit engine, while Markets and Airline is the underearning option. On the December 2025 full-year results call, management quantified the split for the first time, saying more than 80% of profit comes from Holiday Experiences and only 15% from Markets and Airline, reframing the whole company as a high-quality, capital-light hotel and cruise compounder strapped to a turnaround airline.

Three things were added over the period: (1) a dividend policy unveiled in December 2025 (a payout of 10% to 20% of underlying earnings per share, with the first EUR 0.10 per share for the 2025 financial year paid at the February 2026 annual meeting), the headline normalization event after years of nothing; (2) an explicit 3% operating-margin target and a multi-year ambition of 7% to 10% operating-profit growth; and (3) AI elevated from a tool to, in the words of chief executive Sebastian Ebel, a way of how we work, with him calling it by May 2026 the most disruptive part of the sector in the world at the moment, framed as a distribution-cost and process story.

What got quietly harder: the clean 7% to 10% growth framing from December 2025 was effectively replaced by an absolute EUR 1.10 billion to EUR 1.40 billion operating-profit range after the April 2026 guidance change, with management itself admitting in May 2026 that the visibility is not as much as they are used to. The Iran and Strait of Hormuz conflict (two cruise ships trapped for 10 weeks, repatriation flights, about EUR 40 million of second-quarter cost plus a further EUR 20 million) and the Jamaica hurricane (Riu and Royalton hotels closed) are presented as one-off external shocks, which is credible, but they land precisely as the airline turnaround was supposed to inflect.

## 2. Claims versus the financials

| Management claim | Quarter | What the financials say | Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Record performance, fourth consecutive year of significant profit growth | December 2025 | Net income: EUR 306 million in 2023, EUR 507 million in 2024, EUR 636 million in 2025; earnings per share EUR 0.75, EUR 0.98, EUR 1.14 | True. A genuine, steady profit recovery out of the COVID hole (a 2021 net loss of EUR 2.48 billion). |
| Record hotels and cruise results, 84% to 88% occupancy | August and December 2025 | Gross margin only 9.2%, net only 2.6% | Mixed. The segment economics are real, but the consolidated business is razor-thin; the occupancy headlines flatter a 2.6%-net-margin company. |
| Net debt improved another EUR 0.5 billion year over year, 0.6 times earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortization | February 2026 | Live balance sheet: EUR 4.47 billion total debt minus EUR 1.46 billion cash equals EUR 3.01 billion net debt; Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4 (distress) | Materially diverges. Management's roughly EUR 1.3 billion net debt at 0.6 times excludes leases and uses an underlying definition. On a funded-debt basis the balance sheet is still distressed (Altman-Z of 1.4, below the 1.8 danger line). Do not carry the EUR 1.3 billion figure into a valuation. |
| Path to a 3% operating margin, 7% to 10% operating-profit growth | December 2025 | Net margin 2.6%; consensus forward earnings-per-share growth of 31%, but returns on invested capital of 0.4% | Aspirational. The 3% operating margin is a target, not a run-rate; returns on invested capital of 0.4% mean the capital base earns essentially nothing today. |
| Start immediately with a dividend | December 2025 | EUR 0.10 per share declared, paid at the February 2026 annual meeting | True but tiny, about a 1.4% yield at EUR 7; a symbol, not a cash-return thesis. |
| Strong cash flow implied by deleveraging talk | February and May 2026 | Trailing free cash flow of EUR 722 million, but free cash flow down 17% year over year and down 14% a year over three years; 3.7 times free cash flow | Poorer than the narrative. Free cash flow is shrinking even as operating profit grows; capital spending (the Boeing MAX fleet roll, Marella ships, the hotel pipeline) and working-capital and lease dynamics absorb it. The 3.7-times multiple rests on a falling cash flow line. |
| Return on equity of 99% (from the screener) | — | The 99% return on equity sits on a thin, near-wiped equity base after COVID; returns on invested capital are 0.4% | Misleading metric. The 99% return on equity is an artifact of a tiny equity base, not franchise quality; the 0.4% return on invested capital is the honest read. |

In sum, the operational recovery is real and corroborated (revenue of EUR 20.7 billion, then EUR 23.2 billion, then EUR 24.2 billion; the earnings-per-share recovery; beats in 5 of 7 quarters). But the balance-sheet and cash-flow narrative is richer than the numbers: net debt is about EUR 3 billion, not about EUR 1.3 billion, on a funded basis, free cash flow is falling, and returns on capital are near zero.

## 3. Tone and confidence over time

The trajectory is rising into December 2025, then turning defensive and cautious by May 2026.

- August 2025 (third quarter): peak offense, with the quarter delivering the best-ever result and guidance raised to 9% to 11% at constant currency. Expansive talk of new source markets, the Tours product, and the app and AI.
- December 2025 (full year): confident, milestone tone, with an excellent year, record performance, the dividend reinstated, and a 3% margin path laid out. Still on offense, but with hedges introduced on Germany (Ebel: I don't see any positive impact to any business in Germany) and winter (prediction is more difficult than it has been in recent years, not going into a risk strategy).
- February 2026 (first quarter): steady, with the highest underlying operating profit in this quarter and guidance reaffirmed, but the Melissa and Jamaica one-offs introduced.
- May 2026 (second quarter and first half): clearly more defensive, with someone took away the cream on the cake, the visibility is not as much as we are used to, the EUR 1.1 billion to EUR 1.4 billion range wide by management's own admission, and repeated emphasis on being risk-averse, cautious, and careful about investments next year. The language shifts from growth and market share to protecting margin, cutting cost, and capacity discipline (another 1 or 2 of volume with lower margin doesn't help us).

The pivot from more customers, more products in August to protect margin, fill our own assets, be risk-averse in May is the single clearest tonal tell in the set.

## 4. How reliable is guidance

Reliability: medium.

- On the positive side, the August 2025 guidance was raised and the 2025 full-year record was delivered in December 2025. The beat rate is 5 of 7 quarters (71%), and 14 consecutive quarters of underlying operating-profit growth is a real track record.
- On the negative side, the clean 7% to 10% operating-profit growth framing given in December 2025 was changed in April 2026 to an absolute EUR 1.10 billion to EUR 1.40 billion band, a de facto trim and widening forced by the Iran war and the Jamaica hit, then reconfirmed in May. So within about four months the 2026 figure went from a tidy growth rate to a range of plus or minus EUR 150 million that management itself calls low-visibility. That is not a clean beat-and-raise cadence; it is a guide, then revise, then reaffirm pattern. The one-offs are genuine and external, but the episode shows the 2026 path is fragile to outside shocks the company cannot hedge (geopolitics, weather, a late-booking consumer).

As for sandbagging, there is no evidence of conservative-then-beat gaming; if anything the original 2026 framing was optimistic and had to come down.

## 5. Where analysts keep pressing

Recurring, unresolved pushes:
- Cruise pricing. Bernstein (Clarke) and Bank of America repeatedly ask why prices are only flat to up 1% despite roughly 16% to 45% capacity growth and occupancy above 100%. Ebel concedes candidly: no one and especially not me anticipated that the demand would be stronger than the capacity increase, we could probably have risen the price by 3% to 4% more. In other words, they under-priced and left money on the table; the upside is real, but the admission shows pricing discipline lagged demand.
- The Markets and Airline profit bridge. UBS (Nedelcu) presses hard for the moving parts of strong growth on the roughly EUR 200 million Markets and Airline base against down summer bookings (-2%) and wage and overhead inflation. Management answers in transformation generalities (commercialization, the new distribution capability, cost-program detail in December) rather than a quantified bridge, a persistent show-me on the airline turnaround.
- Dividend size versus the cruise joint-venture cash. Bernstein notes the TUI dividend is below the dividend TUI receives from the TUI Cruises joint venture, meaning the parent is paying out less than one joint venture remits to it. Chief financial officer Mathias Kiep deflects to balancing deleverage, investment, and returns. The implicit signal is that cash is still earmarked for the balance sheet, not shareholders.
- Payment terms and working capital. UBS flags UK online travel agents competing on payment terms; Ebel resists strongly (the last escape, I would do my utmost to not agree), revealing competitive pressure on the consumer-cash float that funds the model.

## 6. Warning signs and encouraging signs

Warning signs:
- Funded net debt of about EUR 3.0 billion and an Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4 (distress band); management's EUR 1.3 billion at 0.6 times is a softer, lease-excluding definition, and the more conservative funded read is the honest one.
- Free cash flow is falling (down 17% year over year, down 14% a year over three years) even as operating profit rises; the heavy capital-spending cycle (60 of 100 MAX aircraft still to roll, Marella ships, a 70-hotel pipeline) consumes the recovery, and the 3.7-times free cash flow multiple rests on a declining line.
- Guidance for the 2026 financial year was widened and revised under external shocks, with management-admitted low visibility; the late-booking consumer and discretionary European holiday demand are softening (summer bookings down 2%, average selling price up 3%).
- Cruise, the best segment, is a joint venture (TUI Cruises is 50/50 with Royal Caribbean), so much of its value is equity-method and its cash reaches TUI as a dividend rather than consolidated cash flow.

Encouraging signs:
- A genuine, multi-year operational recovery: net income from EUR 306 million to EUR 636 million, 14 straight quarters of operating-profit growth, beats in 5 of 7 quarters.
- Holiday Experiences is structurally attractive: 84% to 88% hotel occupancy, roughly 100% cruise occupancy, a 35-million-customer first-party database, cruise returns on invested capital of 22% and 17%, and capital-light hotel expansion on a management-fee model.
- Self-help levers remain unspent: airline commercialization (only just rolled out in Belgium and the Netherlands, then the UK), the single technology-platform cost program (detail due December 2026), and under-priced cruise yield, all real, identified upside not yet in the numbers.
- The dividend has been reinstated, a credibility and normalization signal after years of nothing, even if tiny.

## 7. Signals beneath the surface

- A vocabulary shift toward risk capacity and risk-averse. By February to May 2026, management repeatedly frames the airline and tour-operator book around reducing risk capacity and protecting margin rather than winning share, a defensive posture dressed as strategy. Ebel: increased market share would have meant increased losses, that's why we focus very much on the 4- and 5-star differentiated product. Honest, but it concedes the mass-market, undifferentiated end is a loss-maker they are retreating from (the FTI comparison is pointed).
- Aircraft buy-backs from lessors are reframed as currency and financing optimization (buy aircraft when the euro is strong), which moves assets onto the balance sheet, adds funded debt, and is explicitly described as having no other benefits than operational; in other words, do not credit a profit tailwind yet.
- AI elevated from an efficiency tool to existential framing within two quarters, partly genuine (distribution cost, app conversion) but also a narrative upgrade that conveniently arrives as the airline bridge gets harder to quantify.
- The finance chief's careful lease and capital-spending caveat (aircraft delivered by Boeing directly move onto the balance sheet if leased or asset-financed) is a quiet admission that headline net debt understates the true funded obligation.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

Classification: stable and modestly strengthening operationally, but defensively pivoting in posture, on a distressed balance sheet. The operating recovery is real and management is allocating sensibly (deleverage first, capital-light hotels, fleet renewal, a token dividend). But the equity's defining feature is that a high-quality Holiday Experiences franchise is fused to a thin-margin tour-operator and airline and a still-distressed balance sheet of about EUR 3 billion net debt (Altman-Z bankruptcy-risk score of 1.4), with free cash flow falling through the investment cycle.

The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: the cheapness (3.7 times free cash flow, positive margins of safety on both the discounted-cash-flow and earnings-power methods) is conditional on the consumer and the airline turnaround, not on a hard catalyst, and the headline catalysts (the dividend reinstatement, the August 2025 guidance raise) have already fired, while the market is already bullish (EUR 10.4 targets, about 46% implied upside, Jefferies just back to neutral after the run). This is a leveraged cyclical recovery at a price the market has already partly discounted, not an under-covered mispricing.

The competitive advantage: an intangible brand plus a genuine vertical-integration and first-party-distribution cost edge in Holiday Experiences (the funnel and the 35-million-customer database are real), but narrow at the group level, with a 9.2% gross margin, 2.6% net margin, and 0.4% returns on invested capital. Returns are recovering off a near-zero base, so the trend is widening from a low base, not a wide and widening franchise. Tour-operating and the airline are commoditized and capital- and fuel-intensive with no durable edge (FTI's failure is the cautionary peer).

The secular threat is manageable, not terminal. Package and leisure travel is cyclical, not structurally shrinking; the online-travel channel shift (Booking and Expedia disintermediating tour operators) is a real long-run pressure, but TUI's owned hotels and cruises and first-party app are a credible defense, and AI is plausibly a help (distribution cost) as much as a threat here. There is no permanent impairment from a secular force; the risk is cyclical demand plus leverage, not obsolescence.

In summary: credibility is moderate, the trajectory is stable, the moat is narrow but widening from a low base, and the secular threat is manageable.
